data.title: Bristol, Pfizer's Eliquis becoming available on Mark Cuban Cost Plus Drug
data.description:
• Bristol Myers Squibb (BMY) and Pfizer's (PFE) blockbuster blood thinner Eliquis (apixaban) will launch on the Mark Cuban Cost Plus Drug website starting April 27.
  • A 30-day supply will cost $345. 
  • The price matches Bristol and Pfizer's existing Eliquis Direct-to-Patient program.
  • Eliquis is approved to reduce the risk of stroke and blood clots in individuals who have atrial fibrillation, as well as deep vein thrombosis and pulmonary embolism.

data.title: Charter outlines $11.4B 2026 CapEx while expecting run-rate spending below $8B after evolution and expansion conclude
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Charter Communications (CHTR) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "During the first quarter, Spectrum Mobile remained the fastest-growing mobile provider in our footprint, and we now have over 12 million mobile lines, including an increase of 370,000 Spectrum Mobile lines in the quarter" (President, CEO & Director Christopher Winfrey). He also highlighted improving video trends, saying, "our video customer losses continued to improve year-over-year" and "In Internet, competition for new customers remains high. In our first quarter, Internet customer loss totaled 120,000."

  • Winfrey framed the operating environment as pressured but long-term oriented: "Cable industry Internet growth has been pressured for several years now given new competition, a challenging housing environment and other factors like mobile substitution. But we remain confident about our ability to win in the marketplace and grow over the longer term." He emphasized customer-experience investments, including that "NPS scores and other service-related metrics now drive a meaningful part of our overall annual incentive structure" and said new AI tools for agents are "driving higher customer satisfaction and reducing call times."

  • Winfrey described near-term go-to-market actions around convergence and messaging, including, "We launched our $1,000 savings guarantee in February" and said a new digital checkout path "is achieving better yield." He also said Charter is migrating customers to newer plans: "Roughly 45% of our residential customers are now in the pricing and packaging launched in late 2024."

  • "In Mobile, we added 368,000 lines, with higher gross additions year-over-year, more than offset by higher disconnects" (Chief Financial Officer Jessica Fischer). She tied the net-add dynamic to competitive subsidies, citing "heavy device subsidy activity by the big telco competitors, including the iPhone 17."

  • Fischer detailed the Cox transaction framing and scale, including: "Transition expenses relating to the pending Cox transaction totaled $24 million in the quarter" and "We now estimate transaction synergies, or run rate operating expense synergies of at least $800 million, and are likely to grow that further."

Outlook

  • "We do continue to plan to grow EBITDA slightly this year with the benefit of the tailwind from political advertising" (CFO Fischer), adding that offer and pricing “tuning” could affect how close results are to that objective.

  • "We haven't made any determination on [a broadband pricing increase] yet" (President, CEO & Director Winfrey), while reiterating, "it's always been our strategy to try to keep prices as low as we can so that we can have enhanced competitiveness."

  • "From an overall Internet ARPU growth perspective, it will be close either way in terms of where we land" (CFO Fischer), citing multiple moving pieces including offer tuning and overall pricing across products.

Financial Results

  • "First quarter capital expenditures totaled $2.9 billion" (CFO Fischer) and she said Charter still expects "total 2026 capital expenditures to reach approximately $11.4 billion." She also stated, "after our evolution and expansion capital initiatives conclude, our run rate capital expenditures should be below $8 billion per year."

  • "First quarter free cash totaled $1.4 billion" (CFO Fischer), and she attributed the year-over-year change to "accelerated timing of capital expenditures in the year, lower EBITDA and higher cash paid for interest."

  • "We finished the first quarter with $94 billion in debt principal" (CFO Fischer). She added, "Our weighted average cost of debt remains at an attractive 5.2%, and our current run rate annualized cash interest is $4.9 billion," and noted buybacks: "we repurchased 4.3 million Charter shares, totaling $963 million at an average price of $225 per share."

  • "We generated a bit under $1.2 billion of net income attributable to Charter shareholders in the first quarter" (CFO Fischer), and she said it was "primarily driven by lower adjusted EBITDA year-over-year."

Q&A

  • Sean Diffley, Morgan Stanley: Asked about the regulatory backdrop for additional cable consolidation; President, CEO & Director Winfrey responded, "we'd like to acquire more cable assets if it can be done in an appropriate price, conditions," but added, "there's nothing that we're looking at today, or doing today other than just finishing the Cox transaction."

  • Craig Moffett, MoffettNathanson: Asked how quickly Cox customers can be moved to Spectrum pricing given higher Cox broadband ARPU; Winfrey said, "the broadband reported ARPU for Cox is going to go down" and described using mobile and video attach to support household economics, while CFO Fischer added, "the EBITDA margin is also not that different from where we sit today."

  • Vikash Harlalka, New Street Research: Asked about further pricing levers and whether broadband ARPU can accelerate; Winfrey said Charter is "constantly evaluating" and noted trials, but "right now, we don't see any reason to change what we're doing," while Fischer said ARPU for the year "will be close either way."

  • John Hodulik, UBS: Asked about pressure from fixed wireless, fiber promos, and satellite; Winfrey said the issue is "top of funnel," acknowledged "continued footprint expansion from cell phone Internet," and on satellite said, "our tracking in data doesn't suggest a significant customer share loss to satellite," while adding Charter is considering potential cooperation/resale concepts.

  • Sebastiano Petti, JPMorgan: Asked if EBITDA ex-transition still grows and about Cox synergy upside; Fischer said, "We do continue to plan to grow EBITDA slightly this year" and described the synergy increase drivers as "procurement synergies, including programming" plus improved visibility into cost baselines.

  • Steven Cahall, Wells Fargo: Asked whether Charter needs to be more aggressive after Comcast’s reported inflection and about movers; Winfrey said, "Are we going to stand still and just hold tight? No, we're not," and framed the core challenge as cutting through on "value and utility," while Fischer said movers "continues to be something that we see as a net benefit to us."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was neutral-to-skeptical, concentrating on Cox integration economics, broadband ARPU trajectory, competitive pressure (fixed wireless/fiber/satellite), and whether Charter must change marketing/packaging to improve broadband trends.

  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more explanatory in Q&A, with repeated emphasis on confidence and execution focus, including "we remain confident" (President, CEO & Director Winfrey) and that the current issue is "top of funnel" rather than churn.

  • Compared with Q4 2025, management continued to stress converged value and service guarantees, but Q1 2026 discussion leaned more into Cox-close timing and synergy magnitude, while Q4 2025 carried more emphasis on new initiatives launching (e.g., Invincible WiFi and the $1,000 guarantee referenced as upcoming then).

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • In Q1 2026, management said, "We've now received all the necessary federal and state approvals that we need to close, except from California" (President, CEO & Director Winfrey), whereas Q4 2025 framed Cox as conditional: "Assuming regulatory approval of Cox, Spectrum will cover over 70 million households" (President, CEO & Director Winfrey).

  • Synergy expectations increased: Q1 2026 stated "at least $800 million" (CFO Fischer) versus Q4 2025 leverage and integration framing focused more on balance sheet posture and reporting changes, not an $800 million figure.

  • Analyst focus shifted from Q4 2025’s heavier emphasis on wireless/MVNO mechanics and video sustainability to Q1 2026’s heavier concentration on Cox migration pace, broadband ARPU implications, and broader consolidation/regulatory positioning.

Risks and Concerns

  • "The operating environment for new sales, in particular, Internet continues to be competitive" (CFO Fischer), citing "expanded fixed wireless competition," "higher mobile substitution," and "ongoing fiber overlap growth."

  • Winfrey flagged that Charter’s results "don't yet reflect" the value proposition due to "the legacy reputation of cable" (President, CEO & Director Winfrey), and the company highlighted supply constraints on a new device: "The upgrade and attach rate was much higher than expected" for Invincible WiFi and Charter had to "prioritize our supply to a smaller audience" (President, CEO & Director Winfrey).

Final Takeaway

Management emphasized that Q1 2026 results reflected continued strong Spectrum Mobile growth, improving video losses, and a competitive broadband environment centered on top-of-funnel pressure. Leadership positioned near-term execution around clearer value messaging, product utility (including Invincible WiFi), and customer-experience improvements, while placing major attention on the pending Cox closing (California remaining) and an updated synergy outlook of at least $800 million. Financial priorities highlighted the expected decline in capital intensity beyond 2026, with 2026 CapEx guided to approximately $11.4 billion and a longer-term run-rate expectation below $8 billion per year once major initiatives conclude.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: First Financial projects 2Q net interest margin of 3.99% to 4.04% as BankFinancial conversion nears
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: First Financial Bancorp. (FFBC) Q1 2026

Management View

  • “The first quarter was a busy one as we closed the BankFinancial acquisition, completed the conversion of Westfield Bank and wrapped up the sale of the BankFinancial multifamily loan portfolio,” said (President, CEO & Director Archie Brown). Brown highlighted adjusted profitability at “$0.77” per share and said the company’s net interest margin was “resilient despite the Fed funds rate cut in December,” adding, “Assuming no short-term rate reductions by the Fed, we expect the margin to remain stable in the near term.”

  • Brown said loan growth optics were shaped by acquisitions and ICRE payoffs: “Loan balances increased slightly for the quarter due to the BankFinancial acquisition,” but “excluding the BankFinancial portfolio, loans declined for the quarter” as “extended payoff pressure in the ICRE portfolio” offset production. He also tied the growth setup to pipelines and normalization of payoffs: “Loan pipelines are very healthy, and we expect strong production in the second quarter” and “payoff activity in ICRE [to] approach more normal levels.”

  • “Our net interest margin remains very strong at 3.99%, increasing 1 basis point during the quarter,” said (Executive VP, CFO & COO James Anderson). Anderson attributed the quarter’s balance-sheet changes to acquisition mix and payoffs, noting “end-of-period loan balances increased $71 million, which included $228 million acquired in the BankFinancial transaction,” partially offset by “a $152 million decrease in ICRE balances.”

Outlook

  • Brown provided second-quarter ranges and key assumptions: “We expect mid-single-digit loan growth on an annualized basis during the second quarter” and “core deposit balances to remain relatively flat compared to the first quarter.” He guided the margin: “we expect it to hold steady in a 3.99% to 4.04% range over the next quarter, assuming no rate cuts.”

  • Brown guided fee and expense lines: “we expect fee income to be between $75 million and $77 million, which includes $14 million to $16 million for foreign exchange and $20 million to $22 million for leasing business revenue,” and “noninterest expenses are expected to be between $151 million and $154 million.” He also framed integration timing: “we’re on pace to achieve our modeled cost savings in the Westfield acquisition and should realize full savings beginning in the third quarter, and we expect full BankFinancial savings to be realized beginning in the fourth quarter.”

  • Versus the prior call’s Q1 outlook language, Brown’s rate-cut framing shifted from “assuming a 25 basis point rate cut in March” (Q4 call) to “assuming no rate cuts” (Q1 call) as the condition behind margin guidance.

Financial Results

  • Anderson reported acquisition-driven and one-time items alongside adjusted results: “Adjusted net income was $80.5 million or $0.77 per share for the quarter,” and the quarter included “an $8.9 million gain on bargain purchase related to the BankFinancial acquisition.” He said adjusted noninterest income was “$75.6 million,” and Brown said it was driven by “record wealth management income, strong client derivative income and record leasing business income.”

  • Credit metrics were described as stable but with one notable driver: Brown said, “Net charge-offs were 35 basis points of total loans and were impacted by one large commercial relationship,” while Anderson added the allowance “decreased slightly during the quarter to 1.36% of total loans” and “we recorded $8.5 million of provision expense.” Anderson also said NPAs “were 44 basis points” of assets.

  • Capital actions and levels were a recurring theme. Brown said, “Capital ratios are strong and continued to climb,” with tangible common equity “increased to 7.9%,” and added, “the Board of Directors authorized a $5 million share repurchase plan.” Anderson reported tangible book value “increased $0.41 to $16.15.”

Q&A

  • Daniel Tamayo, Raymond James: asked what changes to slow ICRE payoffs and timing; (President, CEO & Director Brown) said exits were driven by “property sales,” “go[ing] to the secondary market,” and “larger banks” returning with “aggressive pricing,” and added, “over the course of the second quarter, that will slow.”

  • Tamayo, Raymond James: asked about production and pipeline drivers; (President, CEO & Director Brown) said the “advanced stage pipeline or a late-stage pipeline… is up substantially” and “it’s pretty much across the board.”

  • Tamayo, Raymond James: asked for a Chicago update post-BankFinancial; (President, CEO & Director Brown) said it was “primarily a deposit play,” and outlined hiring plans: “we’ve added some commercial banking talent… wealth advisers… private bankers,” and “we plan to add more bankers.”

  • Brandon Rud, Stephens: asked about deposit costs embedded in NIM guidance; (Executive VP, CFO & COO Anderson) said first-quarter total deposit cost was “1.83%,” with a second-quarter starting point “around 1.80%, 1.81%,” and “we think we can get that down… another 2 or 3 basis points.”

  • Rud, Stephens: asked for the all-in expense run-rate after saves; (Executive VP, CFO & COO Anderson) said the company expects “a stair step down… in the second quarter” and then “relatively flat for the remainder of the year,” as savings are offset by “other investments.”

  • Karl Shepard, RBC: asked if margin can hold for the year without cuts; (Executive VP, CFO & COO Anderson) said loan growth remix could add “a basis point or 2” as securities rotate but “it’s not that much.”

  • Brian Foran, Truist: asked about excess capital and constraints; (Executive VP, CFO & COO Anderson) said the company is “compounding capital at a high rate,” its “typical constraint… is in the TCE ratio,” and it is considering taking the total payout ratio from “in the low 30s” toward “50% to 60%,” while noting buybacks would be “mindful of price” and “if we see a strategic M&A deal, we would prioritize that in front of the buyback.”

  • Foran, Truist: asked about aggressive CRE market structure; (President, CEO & Director Brown) cited a competitor that “eliminated the covenants,” and said pricing is “certainly sub-200 basis points of spread, 170, 180, in some cases, lower,” adding, “we don’t want to give our skis.”

  • Brendan Nosal, Hovde: asked timing of BankFinancial loan sale and earning assets trajectory; (Executive VP, CFO & COO Anderson) said, “The loan sale closed… March 30,” and described using roughly “$400 million” of cash to let higher-cost funding “run out,” rather than adding securities.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was neutral-to-skeptical, with repeated probing on ICRE paydowns, margin durability, and what management would (or would not) do on CRE structure; Foran explicitly pressed on “where you’re seeing yields or spreads get to” and covenant concessions.

  • Management’s tone was positive in prepared remarks and more guarded on competition in Q&A; Brown paired confidence (“Loan pipelines are very healthy”) with caution about structure (“we don’t think is appropriate”) and long-term underwriting comfort (“we want to be happy about it 2 years from now”).

  • Compared with Q4, the current call carried more integration-and-capital execution detail (Westfield conversion completed; BankFinancial conversion imminent) and more explicit discussion of competitive pressure in CRE terms, while maintaining upbeat framing of profitability and fee income resilience.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Guidance language shifted on rates and margin: Q4 framed NIM with an assumed March cut, while Q1 guided “3.99% to 4.04%… assuming no rate cuts,” reflecting a change in the key assumption rather than a change in the margin’s role as a focal metric.

  • Integration milestones advanced: Q1 emphasized “successfully completed the conversion of Westfield Bank” and set BankFinancial system conversion for “early June,” whereas Q4 focused on pending integration cadence and savings “materialize later in 2026 once both banks have been fully integrated.”

  • Analyst focus rotated from Q4’s fee-income seasonality and Grand Rapids build-out to Q1’s ICRE payoffs, CRE competitive behavior, Chicago team build, and excess capital deployment via a new $5 million repurchase authorization.

Risks and Concerns

  • Brown flagged macro uncertainty: “there is certainly more uncertainty in the economy due to the impact of the war in Iran,” though he added “our current expectations are for asset quality to gradually improve throughout the year.”

  • Credit and competition risks were discussed with specific pressure points: net charge-offs “impacted by one large commercial relationship,” and Brown described market behavior including “aggressive pricing” and cases where a competitor “eliminated the covenants,” while emphasizing the bank’s intent not to match structures it views as imprudent.

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q1 as a high-activity integration quarter—closing BankFinancial, converting Westfield, and exiting the acquired multifamily portfolio—while keeping the narrative centered on a “very strong” 3.99% net interest margin, resilient fee income, and controlled expenses. For Q2, Brown guided mid-single-digit annualized loan growth and held the margin outlook at 3.99% to 4.04% assuming no rate cuts, alongside $75 million to $77 million of fee income and $151 million to $154 million of noninterest expense, with integration cost saves expected to ramp into the second half of 2026 and capital deployment broadened through a new $5 million share repurchase plan.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Novartis pulls bid to expand Pluvicto label for prostate cancer in EU
data.description:
Novartis (NVS) announced on Friday that, following regulatory feedback, it has withdrawn its EU marketing application for Pluvicto, which aimed at broadening the use of the radiotherapy for a specific type of prostate cancer (mCRPC).

The company’s type II variation application sought a label expansion for Pluvicto in adults with prostate-specific membrane antigen (PSMA)-positive metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC).

Novartis (NVS) said that an expert panel of the EU drug regulator, the European Medicines Agency ((EMA)), had found concerns over its PSMAfore study, which had backed the drug’s U.S. approval for this indication.

According to the company, EMA's Committee for Medicinal Products for Human Use indicated its control arm would not support the marketing bid.

“We are disappointed by this outcome and remain committed to advancing treatment options for people with prostate cancer,” the Swiss drugmaker added.

data.title: Semis look ‘bubbly’ and ‘overbought’ – BofA’s Hartnett
data.description:
The Philadelphia Semiconductor Index (SOX) is currently showing extremely overbought conditions, reaching levels not seen since June 2000, according to Michael Hartnett, chief investment strategist at BofA Global Research.

When measured against its 200-day moving average, the index appears “bubbly,” suggesting the market may be running ahead of itself in a way that historically signals elevated risk.

 

 

 

The index is up 48% year-to-date and up almost 35% just in the past month, crossing the 10,000 mark on Thursday.

BofA analysts also noted that the upside breakout is occurring among “blue collar” semiconductor companies—firms like ON Semiconductor (ON), STMicroelectronics (STM), Microchip Technology (MCHP), and Texas Instruments (TXN).

These companies tend to supply chips for industrial and manufacturing applications rather than cutting-edge consumer electronics. When these stocks surge, it typically signals expectations of strong manufacturing activity ahead.

This kind of breakout historically implies the US ISM manufacturing index could rise above 60, a level indicating robust economic expansion. Semiconductor strength often serves as a leading indicator for the broader economic cycle.

“Up in chips” means being “up in cyclicals,” Hartnett wrote.

 

 

 

Semiconductor ETFs: (SMH), (SOXX), (SOXL), (FTXL), (XSD), (USD), (PSI), and (SEMI).

data.title: Universal outlines $352M multiyear reinsurance coverage through 2027-2028 treaty period
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Universal Insurance Holdings (UVE) Q1 2026

Management View

  • CEO Stephen Donaghy said the company “had a fantastic start to the year with a 38.5% annualized adjusted return on common equity,” adding that “top line results were strong with growth across our multistate footprint, including in Florida.”

  • CEO Donaghy also highlighted reinsurance execution, saying, “I’m pleased to announce the completion of our 2026-2027 reinsurance renewal for our insurance entities as our program is now fully supported and secured,” and added, “during the renewal process in 2026, we also secured $352 million of additional multiyear coverage, taking us through the 2027-2028 treaty period.”

  • CFO Frank Wilcox said, “Adjusted diluted earnings per common share was $2 compared to an adjusted diluted earnings per common share of $1.44 in the prior year quarter,” and attributed the change primarily to “a lower net loss ratio and higher net investment income.”

Outlook

  • Management did not provide formal EPS, revenue, premium, or combined ratio guidance ranges in the prepared remarks or Q&A. CEO Donaghy said the company “haven’t kicked off our rate analysis at this point,” and that the team will “analyze the past 12 months and see how that impacts.”

  • On reinsurance, CEO Donaghy indicated the company would defer pricing commentary: “From a pricing perspective, we’re going to sit on that until we get to May and release all the details as normal.”

  • CFO Wilcox confirmed key structural elements for the program: “The retentions will remain the same for the insurance entities, $45 million,” and “we plan to continue to use the captive in the same manner for the $66 million layer above $45 million for the first event.”

Financial Results

  • CFO Wilcox reported “core revenue of $398.2 million,” “direct premiums written were $506.5 million,” “direct premiums earned were $531.4 million,” and “net premiums earned were $356.9 million.”

  • CFO Wilcox reported profitability and underwriting metrics, including “the net combined ratio was 89.7%,” “the 63.9% net loss ratio,” and “the net expense ratio was 25.8%.”

  • On capital return, CFO Wilcox said, “During the first quarter, the company repurchased approximately 210,000 shares at an aggregate cost of $7.1 million,” and added that the repurchase program had “approximately $13.1 million remaining.” He also said, “On April 10, 2026, the Board of Directors declared a quarterly cash dividend of $0.16 per share.”

Q&A

  • Jon Paul Newsome, Piper Sandler & Co., Research Division: asked for “color on the competitive environment, both in Florida and outside of Florida” and whether large ROEs could attract new entrants; CEO Stephen Donaghy responded that Universal is “chasing rate adequacy more than we are chasing business,” adding, “there is competition everywhere, but we feel good about our position and our relationship with our agents has never been stronger.”

  • Jon Paul Newsome, Piper Sandler & Co., Research Division: asked, “Should we expect further price adjustments and rate adjustments for you folks in the future?”; CEO Donaghy said, “We haven’t kicked off our rate analysis at this point,” and added, “we will do the right thing by our shareholders and our partners.”

  • Jon Paul Newsome, Piper Sandler & Co., Research Division: asked about capital management priorities and whether to expect further repurchases; CFO Frank Wilcox responded, “I think we’re going to stay the course,” and said the “#1 priority” is “to support the insurance entities, ensuring that they are adequately capitalized.”

  • Nicolas Iacoviello, Dowling & Partners Securities, LLC: asked for “additional details or commentary” on the reinsurance renewal outcome; CEO Donaghy said the program is “fully secured for 2026, ’27,” and added, “we were very pleased with the market and very pleased with our partners.”

  • Nicolas Iacoviello, Dowling & Partners Securities, LLC: asked how to think about retention and captive usage; CFO Wilcox replied, “The retentions will remain the same for the insurance entities, $45 million,” and said the program is “structurally identical to last year.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst sentiment was neutral-to-slightly positive, with repeated competitive-market questions and follow-ups on pricing, capital priorities, and reinsurance structure, alongside acknowledgments such as “Congratulations on the quarter” and “Congrats on the quarter.”

  • Management sentiment was slightly positive and controlled; CEO Donaghy framed performance as “a fantastic start to the year,” while being cautious on forward specifics, saying reinsurance pricing details would wait until May and that rate analysis has not started.

  • Versus the prior quarter, the current call’s tone remained upbeat but included more explicit deferral on reinsurance pricing (“sit on that until we get to May”) and less detailed discussion on regulatory dynamics than the prior quarter’s emphasis on Florida reforms.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Reinsurance narrative progressed from Q4’s “well underway in negotiating and placing our 2026 reinsurance program” to Q1’s statement that the 2026-2027 renewal is “fully supported and secured,” alongside the added disclosure of “$352 million of additional multiyear coverage” extending to the 2027-2028 treaty period.

  • Growth mix shifted: Q1 included “4.9% growth in Florida and 18.3% growth in other states,” compared with Q4’s “a 3.1% decrease in Florida” and “18.2% growth in other states.”

  • The same core analyst topic persisted across both quarters—competitive conditions—while Q4 also featured a direct regulatory/affordability question, whereas Q1 centered more on reinsurance specifics (including retention/captive structure) and capital deployment priorities.

Risks and Concerns

  • CEO Donaghy emphasized pricing discipline in competitive markets, saying the company is “chasing rate adequacy more than we are chasing business,” and indicated future rate actions remain dependent on a forthcoming review of experience.

  • Management limited near-term visibility on reinsurance pricing, with CEO Donaghy stating, “we think it’d be premature for us to kind of make public comments,” while CFO Wilcox addressed structural continuity by confirming the $45 million retention and continued captive usage for the $66 million layer above it.

Final Takeaway

Management characterized Q1 as a strong start, highlighting a “38.5% annualized adjusted return on common equity,” premium growth “across our multistate footprint,” and completion of a “fully supported and secured” 2026-2027 reinsurance renewal, including “$352 million” of additional multiyear coverage extending through the 2027-2028 treaty period. In Q&A, analysts focused on competitive dynamics, potential rate actions, capital deployment priorities, and reinsurance structure, while management emphasized rate adequacy discipline, a “stay the course” capital framework centered on insurance-entity capitalization, and a plan to provide fuller reinsurance pricing detail in May.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Sanofi, Arrowhead, Novartis among winners of EU recommendations this week
data.description:
An expert panel of the EU drug regulator, the European Medicines Agency ((EMA)), endorsed five new medicines for approval this week, including an autoimmune drug from Sanofi (SNY), a rare disease therapy from Arrowhead Pharma (ARWR), and a gene therapy from Novartis (NVS).

Sanofi

Sanofi (SNY) announced on Friday that the EMA’s Committee for Medicinal Products for Human Use ((CHMP)) recommended the approval of Cenrifki, a treatment targeting an autoimmune disorder known as multiple sclerosis.

Issuing a positive opinion, the CHMP has endorsed the oral Bruton’s tyrosine kinase inhibitor for patients with secondary progressive multiple sclerosis who have not experienced relapses over the last two years, the French drugmaker stated.

The CHMP recommendation will next be reviewed by the European Commission ((EC)) for a final decision on marketing authorization expected in the coming months.

Arrowhead Pharma

Arrowhead Pharmaceuticals (ARWR) announced a positive opinion from the CHMP for its RNA (siRNA) medicine Redemplo (plozasiran) to reduce triglycerides in adults with familial chylomicronemia syndrome, a rare disease.

The Pasadena, California-based biotech expects a final decision on the marketing authorization in Q2 2026.

Novartis

Novartis (NVSEF) announced on Friday that the CHMP recommended approval for its gene therapy, Itvisma, for patients with spinal muscular atrophy, a rare genetic neuromuscular disorder. The panel’s positive opinion endorses the one-time therapy for use in those aged two years and older with a specific type of spinal muscular atrophy.

The company expects a final decision on the authorization in roughly two months.

data.title: S&P 500, Nasdaq rise, Dow lags after Israel-Lebanon ceasefire extension
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages were mixed on Friday as investors digested the latest developments in the Middle East, including a three-week extension to the truce between Israel and Lebanon.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was last +0.4%, while the heavy-tech Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was +1%. Meanwhile, the blue-chip Dow was -0.4%.

Treasury yields were lower. The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) was 2 basis points lower at 4.31%, while the 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) dropped 6 basis points to 3.79%.

U.S. President Donald Trump announced that the ceasefire between Israel and Lebanon will be extended for another three weeks after hosting ambassadors of both countries in Washington. 

"The meeting went very well! The United States is going to work with Lebanon in order to help it protect itself from Hezbollah. The ceasefire between Israel and Lebanon will be extended by THREE WEEKS," he said via Truth Social.

Trump also ordered the U.S. Navy to “shoot and kill” any vessel found placing mines in the Strait of Hormuz, adding that there will be "no hesitation."

Moreover, Iran’s Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps navy laid more mines in the Strait of Hormuz this week, according to a report by Axios.

Crude oil futures (CL1:COM) stayed higher at $95, while Brent (CO1:COM) was last around $105 per barrel.

On the economy, the Consumer Sentiment Index was revised up in April—to 49.8 from the initial 47.6 and 53.3 in March—according to the University of Michigan Survey of Consumers.

data.title: Bets on when Warsh will be confirmed as Fed jumped as DOJ announced drop of criminal probe against Powell
data.description:
Bets on when Kevin Warsh will be confirmed as Fed Chair spiked after the news that the Department of Justice is dropping the criminal probe of current Fed Chair Jerome Powell.

According to predictions market Kalshi (KALSHI), bets that Warsh will be confirmed before June 1 jumped to 98.5%—up from about 60% earlier on Friday—while bets that he will be confirmed before May 15 are now at 84%, up from about 31% on Thursday.

 

 

 

The Department of Justice will drop its criminal investigation into Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell, ending a standoff that threatened to delay the confirmation of Powell’s successor, according to District of Columbia U.S. Attorney Jeanine Pirro on Friday.

The probe, which centered on alleged cost overruns at the Fed’s $2.5B Washington headquarters renovation project, will be referred to the Federal Reserve’s independent inspector general for review.

The closure resolves a legal battle that included D.C. District Judge James Boasberg’s ruling last month tossing out subpoenas Pirro had sent to Powell. Despite the investigation’s end, Pirro made clear the matter is not entirely settled.

“Note well, however, that I will not hesitate to restart a criminal investigation should the facts warrant doing so,” he said.

Powell, whose term ends next month, had previously revealed the investigation in January and called it an attempt by the Trump administration to pressure the Fed to lower interest rates.

More on the Federal Reserve

  • Fed chair nominee Warsh's inflation strategy could backfire - BofA
  • U.S. SEC, Treasury seek more data on private credit risks - report
  • Trump keeps pressure on Powell as Warsh faces lawmakers
  • DOJ closes Powell probe as Fed watchdog takes over cost review
  • Fed chair nominee seeks policy reforms, new framework, new communications

data.title: GATX declares $0.66 dividend
data.description:
• GATX (GATX) declares $0.66/share quarterly dividend, in line with previous.
  • Forward yield 1.33%
  • Payable June 30; for shareholders of record June 15; ex-div June 15.
  • See GATX Dividend Scorecard, Yield Chart, & Dividend Growth.
